# Preface {.unnumbered}

Most PyTorch tutorials explain operations one at a time. Most descriptions of
tabular foundation models begin from a paper or a high-level estimator API.
There is a gap between those two views: the ability to recover the intended
model from a real tensor program and determine whether the implementation
actually preserves that intent.

This book is designed around that gap.

## One model, developed repeatedly

The manuscript follows an educational model called `MiniTFM`. It begins as a
small table-shaped tensor program. Later chapters add target representations,
attention, synthetic tasks, pretraining, probabilistic outputs, and systems
features.

The model is deliberately smaller than a production TFM. Its purpose is to make
important decisions visible:

- what a row, column, task, and query axis means;
- where observed labels enter the model;
- why query labels must remain unavailable;
- how pretraining across tasks differs from fitting one dataset;
- which optimizations alter only execution and which can alter behavior.

Production systems are then studied as case studies, not treated as unexplained
sources of truth.

## How to use the book

Each chapter contains:

- a concrete reader outcome;
- a running tensor contract;
- mathematical and implementation views of the same idea;
- failure cases and tests;
- exercises that require explanation or implementation.

Read with a Python environment available. Run the examples, change the shapes,
break the boundaries, and make the tests catch the breakage.

## A note on a fast-moving field

Specific tabular foundation models will change faster than the underlying
reasoning tools. The main text therefore emphasizes durable concepts.
Version-specific architecture and repository analyses are clearly marked as
case studies and record the model version or commit they describe.
